title: 第 23 節
en: Il8 WHAT ARE THE DEVAS ? [chap.

visible things, were never meant simply for the material fire, for the fiery globe, or for the rosy light of the morning, that appeared and vanished every day. As soon as they were used mythologically, they stood for ideas framed by men who not only saw and stared, but who thought and adored. Agni was not confined to the hearth, but wherever there was light or warmth, whether on earth or in heaven, there was Agni. He was there from the beginning, and he was in these many places, not, as is generally supposed, as the result of a philosophical syncretism, but in consequence of his unbroken manifestation under various forms. Nor was even Surya, the sun, confined to the sky. As SavitW he was supposed to pervade all living things, as Vishnu he stepped across the air, as Mitra he was the delight of the whole world. It seems almost absurd that we should have to insist on these plain facts, but from the way in which some scholars speak of gods and heroes and ancestral spirits, one would almost think that these beings had some substantive existence, that they had lived in India, and had migrated through the clouds to Persia, to Asia Minor, Greece, and Italy, to say nothing of Ilussia, Germany, and Gaul.
True Meaning of Deva.

We should always remember that if the iiishis called the sky, the sun, the moon, and the dawn deva, it is we who have translated this word which meant originally bright, by god. If we could ask the Vedic Rishis what they really meant by calling a number of physical phenomena, or the agents behind them, devas, they would probably n] TRUE MEANING OF DEVA. Iig find the same difficulty in answering such a question which the Greeks felt when they were asked why they called Zeus and Apollo gods, 6eol, nay, which the Jews might feel, if asked why they called Jehovah God, or the Christians when asked why they gave the same name not only to the Father, but also to the Son and to the Holy Ghost. These are questions which it is easy to ask, but which it is almost useless to attempt to answer. We must be satisfied with what history teaches us, with the fact that the Yedic poets called sky, sun and moon, dawn and fire, at first deva, bright, and that afterwards they extended that name, in a more abstract sense, to other phenomena of nature, such as the earth, water, storm, rain, nay even the night, though they certainly were not bright, so that deva in the end meant something indefinable which all these agents shared in common. Whenever the ancients speak of or to these Devas, all we are justified in saying is that they conceived them as bright agents1, without asking as yet any further questions. We cannot say that the Devas were conceived from the first as men, or as animals, or as spirits, or as ghosts, or as fetishes, or as totems, at least there is no tangible evidence in support of any of these views. These gods were simply agents, though they were soon spoken of, even in the Veda, as possessing heads and arms and legs and eyes and ears. One can understand that it would be difficult to define what kind of beings the Greeks thought Zeus and Apollon and Athene to have been. But anybody who knows the Veda would not hesitate for 1 Gifford Lectures, vol. ii, p. 132.
120 TRUE MEANING OF DEVA. [cHAP.

one moment to say that when the ifo'shis addressed Dyaus, they meant the sky in all its aspects, but always as a subject, as active, as an agent. When they spoke of Ushas, they thought of her not only as an agent, but, in addition to that, as a female agent. When they invoked the Maruts, they meant a whole class of active beings manifested in thunder, lightning, and rain. Here lies the immense advantage of the Veda.

Mitra and Varuna. For even if we go a step further and ask what was meant by names no longer used in their appellative character, such as Mitra, Varuna, Rudra, and all the rest, we can gather from the predicates applied to them that Mitra was originally the agent of the bright morning, Varuna of the evening sky, and Rudra of the thunderstorm. Only we must not restrict the sphere of activity of these Devas too narrowly, by translating their names by words which with us have been restricted to a much more narrower sphere. Mitra represents not only the morning sun, but the light of the morning, the day in all its brightness, while Varuna is meant not only for the covering sky, but for the evening or the night, nay for the setting sun ; it may be even for the moon with the stars, as integral parts of the covering sky1. When the sun had once been called the light of day or the eye (£akshus) of Mitra or of the bright Devas 2, the moon would soon be called the bright- 1 II. viii, 555, wj 8' or iv ovpavai liar pa (paavijv dp(p\ o-ihi]vr)v.
Ahuramazda (an original Varuna) says of himself even in so late a work as the Bundehesh, xxx, 5, ' When by me sun and moon and stars are conducted in the firmament (andarvai). '
2 Maitr. Sa7wh. IV, 2, 1, Asau va adityo devanam fcikshus.

n] MITRA AND VARUVA. 121 ness of the night, or the eye of Varwia and the departed spirits 1. All this is intelligible, if we do not attempt to define too much, if we do not ask questions such as would never have presented themselves to the minds of the Vedic Aryas.
The Names of the Devas in Modern Sanskrit, or in Zend.

Another great advantage which the Veda offers to students of mythology is this, that even words which have become mere names, such as Mitra and Varuna, often disclose their etymological meaning either in later Sanskrit, or in the closely allied dialect of ancient Persia. Thus mitra, m., may still be used in ordinary Sanskrit for the sun, and mitra-udaya is the commonest word for sunrise. In Zend, Mithra is represented as the lord of wide pastures with ten thousand eyes2. Four heavenly steeds, white and shining, carry him forward, and as he represents the light of the morning, being bright and clear himself, he is supposed to see and to know everything, and is called the destroyer of darkness, and of the powers of darkness, such as Yatus, Pairikas, &c, the protector of truth and the avenger of untruth. Even when in later times the worship of Mithra had been imported into Italy, we find inscriptions such as ' Deo invicto Soli Mithrae,' showing that the solar beginnings of the god were not quite forgotten, even in foreign countries. Mitra begins with the sun and ends with the sun (mihr in modern Persian is the sun), and though Varuna cannot be analysed 1 £ankh. Sraut. Sutra III, 16, 2, Jiandrama vai pitnwam A'akshus.
2 See Mihir Yasht, S.B.E., vol. xxiii, p. 119.
122 NAMES OF THE DEVAS. [chap.

with the same completeness, still being clearly the complementary deity of Mitra, we cannot doubt for one moment that he was conceived from the first as the dark covering sky, as the evening, as the West, and therefore as occasionally connected with sun and moon also. It is idle to ask such questions as how the sun can be Mitra and likewise the eye of Mitra, and how the moon can be the eye of the Fathers, and at the same time the abode of the Fathers ? In Vedic literature we have to deal with independent poets, every one of whom has a right to think and to speak in his own way, unrestrained as yet by any system. Why then should not one poet call the sun Mitra, and another the eye of Mitra ? Why should not Varuwa be the over-arching sky, and yet be represented as enthroned in the sky, clothed in his cloak and surrounded by his spies ?
Complementary Devas.

Lastly, though originally Vanma was all that Mitra was not, and vice versa, still there was much of the heavenly work, of the return of day and night, that might fall to the share of both gods. Hence they are frequently invoked as a dual deity, as Mitra- Varunau, or even as Mitra, the two Mitras, or Varuna, the two Varu/ias. The sun is then called the eye of both, of Mitra and Varuwa (Rig-veda VII, 61).

All this is perfectly intelligible if we do not refine too much, if we do not imagine that the Veda was built up according to a systematic plan, if we do not perplex ourselves with questions which had no existence in a mythopoeic age. People seem bent on misunderstanding each other. If Mitra is said Il] COMPLEMENTAEY DEVAS. I23 to be the sun, they say at once, surely not the visible, the material sun. If Apollon is called a solar god, they protest that no Greek would ever have recognised Apollon in the sun above their heads. But who ever said so ? When we say that Apollon was the sun, people ought by this time to know what is meant by such an expression, and that no more can be meant than when Louis XIV said, ' L'etat c'est moi.' Even when people call Zeus a solar or Here a lunar deity, they do not mean what Plutarch said (Quaestiones Rom. lxxvii), that Zeus himself in his substance was the sun, and Here herself in her substance was the moon, but simply that the elements from which the character of these deities was elaborated were from the first taken from the sun and the moon. To say that no Greek would have recognised Apollon in the sun, is a strong assertion, considering his names of Phoibos, Xanthos, Chrysokomes, Lykoktonos, Enauros, or in Latin Matutinus l.

Every deity rests on something visible, though it is not that something which is visible, but something invisible within or behind. It is in one sense the infinite behind the finite, the ever-varying object of all religious aspirations ; the agent postulated to account for certain acts, the cause or force postulated to account for certain effects. All these things are known or ought to be known by this time. What still remains for the comparative philologist to do is to prove the presence of the material beginnings of each deity, to lay bare what we call the solar, lunar, vernal, 1 Cf. v. Schroeder, K. Z., xxix, p. 195 ; v. Willamowitz, Hermes, xviii, 406 ; Indogerm. Forschungen, iv, 173.
124 COMPLEMENTARY DEVAS. [chap.

hibernal, or any other ingredients which give to each god his own peculiar character. When we have to deal with gods such as Surya or Helios, the names suffice. But in other cases, such as that of Mitra or Varuna, we must go further and discover for ourselves in some of their epithets, in certain legends told about them or in prayers addressed to them, the true constituent elements of their character, such as it was imagined by their worshipjDers. It is true that when the agents behind the phenomena of nature have once become Devas or gods, they often lose the traces of their physical character ; they are simply conceived as ideal, all-powerful, all-wise beings who are able to reward or to punish the children of men. Thus in many of the verses addressed to Mitra in the Veda, we find him represented no longer as connected with the sun, but as greater than heaven and earth, nay as supporting all the gods, as watching with open eyes over the whole world, and as protecting those who obey his commands \ It may be said that in that case Mitra is no longer the sun, the material and visible sun. He never was that. But is he therefore a fetish, or a totem, or an ancestral spirit ? If we call Mitra the god of the sun, wre use a phrase which no Vedic poet would ever use. He could not speak of a devaA suryasya, a god of the sun. To him the deva Mitra would express the agent within or behind the sun, but whether he would distinguish the agent, as such, from the sphere of his agency, is more than we can say. All we can do, if we wish to understand the hymns of the Big-veda, is to watch the historical process by 1 Eig-veda X, i, 41.

n] COMPLEMENTARY DEVAS. 125 which in the minds of the ancient Aryas the sun, by a kind of involuntary abstraction, became slowly divested of its purely material attributes, and was raised gradually to a higher and higher, nay to a supernatural rank, as the supporter of the world, the bringer of light, the guardian of truth, the avenger of evil, the friend of man.
Mitra and Varcma.

There is unfortunately one hymn only in the Veda addressed to Mitra exclusively. Generally Mitra is so closely united with Vanma, that the two seem to form but one deity \ and it is in that united capacity, as the two Mitras or the two Varunas, that they make the sky to shine, send down rain, look down from heaven, dispel darkness and falsehood, are lords of light and right, righteous themselves, avengers of falsehood, and deliverers from evil. Yet they are distinguished from each other even by the poets who address them in common. VarUna is called the lord, the unconquerable guide, and thus far, the greater of the two ; Mitra is praised as calling man back to his work (Rig-veda VII, 36, 2) in the morning. If in the compound name Mitra always stands first, this may be due either to a recollection that as representing the rising sun Mitra was originally the principal and more important partner, but it may also be due to the well-known fact that in all Dvandvas the shorter word comes first (Pan. II, 2, 34).
In the Avesta the name of Vanma has vanished, but his place as the twin companion of Mithra has been taken by no less a deity than Ahura (Mazda)
1 Muir, Original Sanskrit Texts, v, 68.
126 MITKA AND VARLLVA. [cHAP.

himself1. It would be wrong to say that the Veclic VaruTia has become Ahura Mazda in the Avesta. No individual god ever becomes another individual god, and there are things ascribed to Ahura Mazda which were never ascribed to Vanma. Thus Ahura Mazda is said to have created Mithra, a paternity never claimed by Vanma. But the place and position of the Asura Vanma as the most powerful and sometimes supreme deity has certainly been taken in the Avesta by him who is called Ahura koj lt;oyr]v, that is Ahura Mazda or Ormazd. Mithra is in that case lord of wide pastures, who has a thousand ears and ten thousand eyes, and he is invoked with Ahura just as Mitra was invoked with Vanma. Thus we read in the Mihir Yasht, XXVIII, 113,' May Mithra and Ahura (instead of Mitra- Varwiau in the Veda 2), the high gods, come to us for help ;' and again, XXXV, 145, 'We sacrifice unto Mithra and Ahura, the two great, imperishable, holy gods ; ' and Khorshad Nyayish 6, ' We sacrifice unto the bright, undying, shining, swift-horsed Sun ; we sacrifice unto Mithra, the lord of wide pastures, who is truthspeaking, a chief in assemblies, with a thousand ears, well shapen, with ten thousand ears, high, with full knowledge, strong, sleepless, and ever awake ; we sacrifice unto Mithra, the lord of all countries, whom Ahura Mazda made the most glorious of all the gods in the world unseen. So may Mithra 1 Though it seems impossible to identify Ahuro mazdiio with the Vedic Asuro vedha/«, on account of the initial consonants m in Zend and v in Sanskrit, their substantial identity can no longer be doubted.
2 Like Mitra, the two Mitras, and Varuwa, the two Varimas, we find in the Avesta also such forms as Ahuraeibya Mithraeibya.

Il] MITRA AND VARUAA. 1 27 and Ahura, the two great gods, come to us for help.' Here we see as it were before our eyes the growth of a solar god, disengaging himself from his physical antecedents, and rising higher and higher to the stage of a moral and purely spiritual being. It might seem even as if a distinction was made between the Sun and Mithra, but that distinction only shows that Mithra had risen above his original cradle, and that therefore he might be said to come before the Sun, and to be the lord of the Sun.
zh: Il8 何謂提婆（Devas）？ [第‧章]

可見之物，從來就不僅僅是指物質的火、熾熱的球體，或是每日出現又消逝的玫瑰色晨光。一旦它們被神話式地使用，便代表了人類所構想的概念——這些人不僅觀看與凝視，更進行思考與崇拜。阿耆尼（Agni）並不侷限於爐灶；無論何處有光或溫暖，無論在地上或天上，那裡就有阿耆尼。祂從太初就在那裡，並且同時存在於許多地方——這並非如一般所認為的，是哲學性綜攝（syncretism）的結果，而是由於祂以各種形式不間斷地顯現所致。即便是太陽神蘇利耶（Surya），也不僅限於天空。作為薩維特爾（Savitṛ），祂被認為遍及一切生物；作為毗濕奴（Vishnu），祂跨越天際；作為密多羅（Mitra），祂是整個世界的喜悅。我們竟必須堅持這些顯而易見的事實，這幾乎顯得荒謬；然而，從某些學者談論神祇、英雄與祖先神靈的方式來看，人們幾乎會以為這些存有具有某種實質的存在，以為他們曾在印度生活，並穿過雲層遷徙至波斯、小亞細亞、希臘與義大利——更不用說伊利裡亞、日耳曼與高盧了。

「提婆」（Deva）一詞在梵文中意為「發光者」或「明亮者」，其詞根 *div* 意指「照耀」。這與希臘文的 *Zeus*（宙斯）、拉丁文的 *Deus*（神）、以及英文的 *deity*（神性）同源。在梨俱吠陀（Rig-Veda）中，提婆最初並非指涉至高無上的神靈，而是指那些顯現於自然現象中的光明力量——如天空、太陽、黎明、火焰等。這些存在被視為「無限者」（the Infinite）的局部顯現，而非獨立的神祇。 穆勒指出，提婆的原始意義揭示了宗教意識的演進：從對自然光輝的敬畏，逐漸發展為對神聖力量的抽象理解。這種轉變體現了「語言科學」（the science of language）如何影響宗教觀念——當最初的隱喻被遺忘時，提婆便從「發光者」固化為「神靈」，進而催生了多神教（polytheism）與後來的單一神教（henotheism）或輪換主神教（kathenotheism）。因此，理解提婆的真正含義，是解開雅利安（Aryan）宗教起源的關鍵。

我們應當時時謹記：若《梨俱吠陀》的仙人稱呼天空、太陽、月亮與黎明為「提婆」（deva），那是我們將這個原意為「光明者」的詞語翻譯成了「神」。倘若我們能詢問吠陀仙人，他們稱呼諸多自然現象（或這些現象背後的動因）為提婆時究竟意指為何，他們恐怕會像希臘人面對「為何稱宙斯與阿波羅為神（θεοί）」的提問時一樣難以作答；甚至猶太人若被問及「為何稱耶和華為神」，或基督徒被問及「為何將同一稱號不僅賦予聖父，也賦予聖子與聖靈」時，也會感到同樣的困惑。這些問題雖易於提出，卻幾乎無從回答。我們必須滿足於歷史的教導：吠陀詩人起初稱呼天空、太陽、月亮、黎明與火焰為「提婆」（光明者），其後他們以更抽象的方式將此名延伸至其他自然現象——如大地、水、風暴、雨水，甚至黑夜（儘管黑夜顯然並不光明）——以致「提婆」最終意指所有這些動因所共享的某種難以界定的特質。古人談論或向這些提婆祈禱時，我們所能合理斷言的僅是：他們將之視為「光明的動因」¹，暫且無須追問更多。我們不能斷言提婆最初被構想為人、動物、靈體、鬼魂、物神或圖騰——至少沒有任何確鑿證據支持這些觀點。這些神祇純粹是動因，儘管即使在《吠陀》中，它們也很快被描述為擁有頭顱、手臂、腿足、眼睛與耳朵。不難理解，要界定希臘人認為宙斯、阿波羅與雅典娜是何種存有實屬困難；但任何熟悉《吠陀》的人都不會遲疑地承認：¹ 吉福德講座，第二卷，第132頁。

120 提婆（Deva）的真正意義。[章節] 在吠陀（Veda）的讚歌中，我們發現「提婆」（Deva）這個詞經常被用來指稱那些被崇拜的神祇。然而，這個詞的原始意義並非「神」，而是「光明者」或「閃耀者」。它源自梵文詞根 *div*，意為「發光」。因此，提婆最初指的是天空中的光明現象，如太陽、月亮、星辰、黎明、閃電等。這些自然現象被視為活生生的存在，並逐漸被賦予人格化的特質，最終成為神靈。這種從自然現象到人格化神祇的演變，正是宗教學（the science of religion）所關注的核心過程之一。穆勒（Müller）指出，這種現象並非雅利安（Aryan）民族所獨有，而是普遍存在於人類宗教的早期階段。通過比較神話學（comparative mythology）的研究，我們可以追溯這些神祇名稱的原始含義，從而理解人類心靈如何從對無限者（the Infinite）的感知中，逐步建構出多神教（polytheism）的體系。
片刻之間，我們可以說，當《梨俱吠陀》的詩人向特尤斯（Dyaus）呼告時，他們所指涉的是天空的一切面向，但始終將其視為一個主體、一個主動者、一個施動者。當他們談論烏莎斯（Ushas）時，不僅將她視為一個施動者，更進一步，視其為一個女性施動者。當他們呼喚摩錄多（Maruts）時，他們所指的是一整類在雷電、閃電與降雨中顯現的主動存在。此處正是《吠陀》的無上優勢所在。

因為即使我們再進一步追問，那些不再以普通名詞性質使用的名稱——如密陀羅（Mitra）、伐樓那（Varuna）、樓陀羅（Rudra）以及其他所有神祇——究竟意指為何，我們仍可從加諸其上的謂詞推斷：密陀羅原為明亮清晨的施動者，伐樓那為傍晚天空的施動者，而樓陀羅則為雷雨風暴的施動者。只是我們絕不可將這些提婆（Devas）的活動範圍限制得過於狹隘，亦即不可將其名稱譯為那些在我們語言中已被限定於更狹隘範圍的詞語。密陀羅不僅代表早晨的太陽，更代表晨光、白晝及其一切光輝；而伐樓那不僅意指覆蓋的天空，更代表傍晚或夜晚，甚至可指落日；它甚至可指伴隨星辰的月亮，因為這些都是覆蓋天空的組成部分¹。一旦太陽被稱為白晝之光，或密陀羅（或明亮提婆²）之眼（£akshus），月亮很快便會被稱為明亮—— ¹《梨俱吠陀》卷八，第555首：「如天上明月之光輝。」 ²《梨俱吠陀》卷十，第37首：「密陀羅之眼。」
阿胡拉‧馬茲達（Ahuramazda，原初的伐樓那〔Varuna〕）甚至在晚近的著作如《班達希申》（Bundehesh）第三十章第五節中，仍自述道：「當太陽、月亮與星辰由我引領於蒼穹（andarvai）之中。」
2 Maitr. Sa7wh. IV, 2, 1, Asau va adityo devanam fcikshus.
夜的黑暗，或伐樓那之眼與離世之靈¹。這一切皆可理解，只要我們不過度嘗試定義，不提出那些吠陀雅利安人心中從未浮現的問題。
提婆在現代梵文或瑣羅亞斯德語中的名稱
¹ Śankh. Śraut. Sūtra III, 16, 2: "Candramā vai pitṝṇāṃ cakṣuḥ"（月亮確實是祖先的眼睛）。
2 參見《密赫爾‧亞什特》（Mihir Yasht），《東方聖書》（S.B.E.）第二十三卷，第119頁。

> 「Deva」一詞，在梨俱吠陀中，其意義遠比我們通常所理解的「神」更為寬泛。它幾乎可用於任何被視為光明、善良或可敬的事物。太陽、月亮、星辰、黎明、蒼穹、大氣、大地、河流、火、水、風——所有這些，以及更多，皆被稱為提婆。然而，這並非意味著吠陀詩人將這些自然現象本身崇拜為神；他們所崇拜的，乃是隱於其後、運作於其中、並透過它們顯現的無限者。因此，當他們呼喚「阿耆尼（Agni）啊！」，或「因陀羅（Indra）啊！」，或「伐樓那（Varuna）啊！」時，他們並非向火焰、雷電或蒼穹祈禱，而是向那在火焰中燃燒、在雷電中轟鳴、在蒼穹中照耀的不可見力量祈禱。這些力量，他們稱之為提婆，意即「光明者」或「照耀者」，此詞與拉丁文的 *deus*、希臘文的 *theos* 同源，皆源於同一雅利安語根 *div*，意為「照耀」。 因此，在吠陀宗教中，我們發現了一種獨特的現象，即穆勒所稱的「單一神教（henotheism）」或「輪換主神教（kathenotheism）」。這並非多神教，亦非一神教，而是介於兩者之間的一種階段。在此階段中，每一位被祈禱的神祇，在祈禱者心中，暫時被視為至高無上的神，擁有其他所有神祇的屬性與權能。當詩人歌頌伐樓那時，伐樓那便是宇宙的創造者、維持者與統治者；當他轉而歌頌因陀羅時，因陀羅便成為同樣至高無上的存在。這種對單一神祇的專注崇拜，並不否定其他神祇的存在，而是將它們暫時置於從屬地位。此即「單一神教」與「輪換主神教」之真諦，它為後來更成熟的「一神教」概念鋪平了道路。

以同樣的完整性，且仍明確為密特拉的補充神祇時，我們一刻也無法懷疑：祂從最初就被構想為黑暗籠罩的天空、為夜晚、為西方，因此也偶爾與太陽及月亮相關。追問諸如「太陽何以既是密特拉，又是密特拉之眼？」、「月亮何以既是先祖之眼，又是先祖的居所？」這類問題，實屬徒勞。在吠陀文獻中，我們面對的是各自獨立的詩人，每一位都有權以自己的方式思考與言說，尚未受任何體系約束。既然如此，為何一位詩人不能稱太陽為密特拉，而另一位詩人卻稱之為密特拉之眼？為何伐樓拿不能既是籠罩穹蒼，同時又被描繪為端坐於天際、身披斗篷、四周環繞著密探？

在吠陀（Veda）的讚歌中，我們經常發現一對對的神祇被同時召喚，彷彿他們構成一個不可分割的整體。這種現象並非偶然，而是反映了早期宗教思維中一種深層的直覺：即個別的神明並非絕對獨立的存在，而是某種更高、更根本力量的互補面向。例如，特尤斯（Dyaus）與大地、阿耆尼（Agni）與蘇摩（Soma）等配對，顯示出這些提婆（Deva）在功能與本質上相互依存。穆勒（Müller）指出，這種互補性正是從多神教（polytheism）過渡到單一神教（henotheism）乃至一神教（monotheism）的關鍵線索。透過比較神學（comparative theology）的視角，我們得以理解：這些看似各自獨立的神明，實則是人類心靈在面對無限者（the Infinite）時，所採用的不同命名與象徵。語言科學（the science of language）更進一步揭示，許多神名最初不過是自然現象的擬人化稱呼，而「語言的疾病」（a disease of language）則使後人誤以為這些名稱代表著截然不同的實體。因此，吠陀中的互補提婆不僅是宗教學（the science of religion）的重要研究對象，也為我們理解雅利安（Aryan）與閃語族（Semitic）宗教的早期形態提供了珍貴的比較材料。

最後，雖然伐樓拿（Varuna）原本是密多羅（Mitra）所不是的一切，反之亦然，但仍有許多天上的工作——晝夜的交替——可能歸於兩位神祇的職責。因此，祂們常被當作一對雙神來祈求，稱為密多羅－伐樓拿（Mitra-Varuṇau），甚至稱為密多羅——兩位密多羅（the two Mitras），或伐樓拿——兩位伐樓拿（the two Varuṇas）。太陽於是被稱為兩者——密多羅與伐樓拿——的眼睛（《梨俱吠陀》VII, 61）。

所有這一切，只要我們不過度鑽研、不設想《吠陀》是按某種系統性計畫建構而成、也不以神話時代尚不存在的問題來困擾自己，便完全能夠理解。人們似乎執意要誤解彼此。若說密陀羅（Mitra）是太陽，他們立刻會說：當然不是指可見的、物質的太陽。若稱阿波羅（Apollon）為太陽神，他們便抗議，說沒有任何希臘人會把頭頂上的太陽當作阿波羅。但誰曾這樣說過？當我們說阿波羅是太陽時，人們到現在理應明白這種表述的含義——其意涵絕不會超過路易十四所說的「朕即國家」（L'état c'est moi）。即使人們稱宙斯（Zeus）為太陽神、赫拉（Hera）為月神，他們的意思也並非如普魯塔克（Plutarch）所言（《羅馬問題》第七十七節），即宙斯本質上就是太陽、赫拉本質上就是月亮，而僅僅是說：構成這些神祇性格的元素，最初取自太陽與月亮。若說沒有任何希臘人會從太陽中認出阿波羅，這可是個大膽的主張——考量到阿波羅的別名包括福玻斯（Phoibos）、克桑托斯（Xanthos）、克呂索科梅斯（Chrysokomes）、呂科克託諾斯（Lykoktonos）、厄瑙羅斯（Enauros），或拉丁文中的馬圖提努斯（Matutinus）。

每一位神祇皆奠基於某種可見之物，儘管其所依憑的並非那可見之物本身，而是其中或背後不可見的某種存在。從某種意義上說，祂是有限者背後的無限者，是一切宗教渴望中不斷變化的對象；是被預設來說明特定行為的動因，是被預設來說明特定結果的原因或力量。這一切，如今應已為人所知，或理應為人所知。比較語文學家仍需完成的工作，在於證明每位神祇物質起源的存在，揭露我們所謂的太陽、月亮、春天¹之本質。 ¹ 參見 v. Schroeder, *K. Z.*, xxix, p. 195；v. Willamowitz, *Hermes*, xviii, 406；*Indogerm. Forschungen*, iv, 173。

124 互補的提婆。[章節標記] 在吠陀中，我們發現一個詞語，其意義與「提婆」截然相反，那就是「阿修羅」（Asura）。「阿修羅」一詞在梨俱吠陀中經常出現，其意義與後期梵文文學中截然不同。在梨俱吠陀中，「阿修羅」意指「神靈」，與「提婆」幾乎同義；它被應用於許多最重要的神祇，如伐樓那（Varuna）、密多羅（Mitra）、阿耆尼（Agni）等。然而，在後期吠陀文獻中，尤其是在奧義書中，我們看到「阿修羅」一詞的意義發生了轉變，它開始意指「惡魔」，與「提婆」相對立。這種轉變是如何發生的？穆勒認為，這與「阿修羅」一詞的詞源有關。「阿修羅」源自詞根 *as*，意為「存在」或「生命」，因此「阿修羅」原意為「有生命的」或「神聖的」。然而，當「提婆」一詞逐漸專指「光明之神」時，「阿修羅」則被用來指稱那些更為抽象、更為深奧的神靈，特別是與自然力量相關的神靈。後來，由於宗教觀唸的演變，特別是當一神教的傾向開始出現時，那些被認為是與至高神對立的神靈，便被貶低為「阿修羅」，即「惡魔」。因此，「阿修羅」與「提婆」的對立，反映了宗教思想從多神教向一神教過渡的過程，也體現了語言在宗教觀念形成中的作用。穆勒將這種現象稱為「語言的疾病」，即詞語的原始意義在歷史發展中被扭曲或遺忘，從而導致了神話和宗教觀唸的混淆。

冬日的、或任何其他賦予每位神祇其獨特性格的成分。當我們處理像蘇利耶（Surya）或赫利奧斯（Helios）這類神祇時，名稱便已足夠。但在其他情況，例如密陀羅（Mitra）或伐樓那（Varuna），我們必須更進一步，從他們的一些別名、關於他們的某些傳說、或向他們祈禱的頌詞中，自行發現其性格的真正構成元素，亦即其崇拜者所想像的那樣。誠然，當自然現象背後的行動者一旦成為提婆（Devas）或神祇，他們往往會失去其物質性格的痕跡；他們僅被構想為理想的、全能的、全知的存在，能夠賞罰人類之子。因此，在《吠陀》中許多獻給密陀羅的詩句裡，我們發現他不再被描繪為與太陽相關，而是比天地更偉大，甚至支撐著所有神祇，睜著雙眼注視整個世界，並保護那些服從其命令的人¹。可以說，在這種情況下，密陀羅不再是太陽，那個物質的、可見的太陽。他從來就不是。那麼，他因此就是一個物神（fetish）、一個圖騰（totem）、或一個祖先之靈嗎？如果我們稱密陀羅為太陽之神，我們使用的是一個任何吠陀詩人都不會使用的詞語。他不會說一個「太陽的提婆」（devaḥ sūryasya），一個太陽之神。對他而言，提婆密陀羅表達的是太陽之內或背後的行動者，但他是否會將行動者本身與其行動的領域區分開來，這遠非我們所能斷言。如果我們想要理解《梨俱吠陀》的讚歌，我們所能做的就是觀察其歷史過程¹。 ¹ 梨俱吠陀 X, 1, 41.
n] 互補的提婆。 125 在古代雅利安人的心智中，太陽經由一種不自覺的抽象作用，逐漸剝離其純粹物質屬性，並逐步被提升至愈來愈高、甚至超自然的地位，成為世界的支撐者、光明的帶來者、真理的守護者、邪惡的報復者、人類的朋友。
米特拉（Mitra）與瓦魯那（Varcma）。

不幸的是，在《吠陀》中僅有一首頌歌是專門獻給密陀羅（Mitra）的。一般來說，密陀羅與伐樓那（Varuna）緊密相連，以致兩者彷彿構成同一位神祇；正是以這種聯合的形態——作為兩位密陀羅或兩位伐樓那——他們使天空發光、降下雨水、從天上俯視、驅散黑暗與虛假，成為光明與正義之主，自身公正無私，懲罰虛假，並拯救世人脫離邪惡。然而，即便是那些共同向他們祈禱的詩人，也將兩者區別開來。伐樓那被稱為「主」、「不可征服的引導者」，就此而言，他是兩者中更偉大的一位；密陀羅則被讚頌為在清晨喚醒人們回去工作（《梨俱吠陀》VII, 36, 2）。若在複合名稱中密陀羅總是居於首位，這或許是由於一種記憶：作為旭日的代表，密陀羅原本是更主要、更重要的夥伴；但也可能源於一個眾所周知的事實：在所有並列複合詞（Dvandva）中，較短的詞總是排在前面（波你尼《梵語語法》II, 2, 34）。
在《阿維斯塔》中，梵瑪（Vanma）之名已然消失，但其作為密特拉（Mithra）孿生伴侶的位置，卻被一位絲毫不遜色的神祇——阿胡拉（馬茲達）（Ahura (Mazda)）所取代。
1 Muir, *Original Sanskrit Texts*, v, 68.
126 密特拉（Mitra）與伐樓那（Varuna）。 [章節]

若說吠陀中的伐樓拿（Varuṇa）在《阿維斯陀》中已成為阿胡拉‧馬茲達（Ahura Mazda），那便錯了。沒有任何個別神祇會變成另一個個別神祇，且《阿維斯陀》中歸於阿胡拉‧馬茲達的某些屬性，從未歸於伐樓拿。例如，阿胡拉‧馬茲達被說成創造了密特拉（Mithra），這種父權從未為伐樓拿所宣稱。然而，阿修羅‧伐樓拿（Asura Varuṇa）作為最強大、有時甚至是最高的神祇，其地位與位置，確實已在《阿維斯陀》中被稱為阿胡拉（Ahura）*mazdā̃*，即阿胡拉‧馬茲達或奧爾馬茲德（Ormazd）者所取代。在該情況下，密特拉是廣闊牧場之主，擁有千耳萬眼，且他與阿胡拉一同被祈請，正如密多羅（Mitra）曾與伐樓拿一同被祈請一般。因此，我們在《密特拉讚歌》（Mihir Yasht）第二十八章第一一三節讀到：「願密特拉與阿胡拉（取代吠陀中的密多羅-伐樓拿¹），這兩位至高神祇，前來助我們一臂之力。」又在第三十五章第一四五節：「我們向密特拉與阿胡拉獻祭，這兩位偉大、不朽、神聖的神祇。」以及《太陽祈禱文》（Khorshad Nyayish）第六節：「我們向光明、不朽、輝煌、快馬如飛的太陽獻祭；我們向密特拉獻祭，他是廣闊牧場之主，言語真實，集會之首領，有千耳，形貌端正，有萬耳，崇高，具足智慧，強健，不眠，且永遠警醒；我們向密特拉獻祭，他是萬國之主，阿胡拉‧馬茲達使他在不可見的世界中成為眾神中最榮耀者。願密特拉¹……」 ¹ 雖然將阿胡拉‧馬茲達（Ahurō mazdå̃）等同於吠陀的阿修羅‧維達（Asurō vedhā）看似不可能，因為瑣羅亞斯德語（Zend）的詞首輔音 *m* 與梵語的 *v* 有別，但它們在本質上的同一性已無可置疑。
如同密多羅（Mitra）、兩位密多羅，以及伐樓拿（Varuṇa）、兩位伐樓拿，我們在《阿維斯塔》中也發現了像 Ahuraeibya Mithraeibya 這樣的形態。
在此處，我們彷彿親眼目睹一位太陽神（solar god）的成長過程：他逐漸脫離其物理根源，步步攀升，直至成為一個道德且純粹靈性的存有。甚至可能有人認為，太陽與密特拉（Mithra）之間有所區別，但這種區別恰恰顯示密特拉已超越其原初的搖籃，因此可以說他先於太陽而來，並為太陽之主。
